category_title: First-grade struggling readers who demonstrate graphosemantic awareness by identifying and rejecting incorrect speech-to-text transcriptions
student_description: Maggie is a highly engaged struggling reader who uses speech recognition (SR) to bypass spelling difficulties but treats dictated text as a final draft. When SR produces errors, she becomes frustrated and deletes entire sentences or drafts rather than editing them. She uniquely requires explicit metacognitive modeling to evaluate transcription errors and physical chart paper modeling to understand drafting as a malleable, multi-step process.

criteria.rationale: Because Maggie tends to delete entire drafts when SR makes a mistake, she needs explicit modeling of how to compare her spoken words to the transcribed text.
criteria.check: The tutor uses a think-aloud strategy to explicitly compare the student's dictated word with the inaccurate transcribed word (e.g., analyzing sounds, letters, or meanings) rather than just telling her it is wrong.

criteria.rationale: SR can occasionally produce inappropriate words (e.g., 'hell' instead of 'hill'), which distracts Maggie and prompts her to delete everything.
criteria.check: If an inappropriate or distressing word is transcribed, the tutor immediately deletes or alters it, or directs the student to do so swiftly without dwelling on the mistake.
